question: Does the NuScale design adequately address station blackout (SBO) given the Ch.01 passive design philosophy and Ch.05 safety system details?

nuscale_ch01_0278.title: SRP Appendix 8-A, Rev1: General Agenda, Station Site VisitsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0278.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with various regulatory criteria related to DC power systems and station blackout requirements. It notes partial conformance with several Regulatory Guides (RGs) and clarifies that some requirements, such as maintenance rule implementation and alternate AC power sources, are either the responsibility of the COL applicant or not applicable to the NuScale design. Additionally, SRP Appendix 8-A is identified as governing staff site visits during licensing reviews, but is not applicable to the design certification stage.

nuscale_ch01_0427.title: DSRS 10.3, Rev 0: Main Steam Supply SystemII.4 MSS is capable of supporting core cooling or safe-shutdown (non-DBA) in the event of an SBO (10 CFR 50.63)
nuscale_ch01_0427.summary: The section reviews the NuScale Main Steam Supply System (MSS) against regulatory criteria, noting that while the MSS is not safety-related, its design ensures backup and protection for safety functions, particularly through secondary isolation valves. Specifically, regarding SBO (Station Blackout) events, the MSS partially conforms to requirements by supporting core cooling and safe shutdown through passive design features and minimal reliance on AC power.

nuscale_ch01_0275.title: DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station BlackoutII.1 Compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 and the guidelines of RG 1.155
nuscale_ch01_0275.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s compliance with regulatory requirements for station blackout (SBO) and related DC power systems. The NuScale design partially conforms to 10 CFR 50.63 and RG 1.155, as all safety-related functions can be performed without AC power for 72 hours after an SBO event, making alternate AC power sources unnecessary. Some requirements, such as maintenance rule implementation, are the responsibility of the COL applicant, and the independence of SBO-related power supplies is addressed in referenced sections.

nuscale_ch01_0023.title: Containment Systems
nuscale_ch01_0023.summary: The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operations, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. In the event of an emergency, passive cooling rapidly reduces pressure and temperature, and the design’s reliance on passive safety systems and small fuel inventory results in improved safety and a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios and reduce reliance on external power, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors. Additionally, the smaller fuel inventory leads to a reduced radioactive source term.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the NuScale Power Plant’s major design features and parameters with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, and system components, showing that NuScale uses smaller, simpler, and more integrated systems compared to conventional PWRs. Tables provide side-by-side comparisons of key parameters and safety system components.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During ECCS activation, steam is vented and condensed within containment, and recirculated water cools the core, with heat ultimately transferred passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0025.title: Control Room Habitability System
nuscale_ch01_0025.summary: The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive safety feature designed to protect plant operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases by supplying clean, compressed air to the main control room (MCR). It maintains positive pressure in protected areas and can provide breathable air for at least 72 hours after an incident, even if AC power is unavailable.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale plant’s core and cooling system operations. It explains how heat generated in the reactor core is transferred via natural circulation to steam generators, producing superheated steam that drives a turbine and generates electricity. The section also notes that site-specific details exist for the cooling water system and cooling towers.

nuscale_ch01_0026.title: Fission Product Removal and Control Systems
nuscale_ch01_0026.summary: The only credited fission product removal and control system in the NuScale design is the containment vessel (CNV) working with the containment isolation system. Fission product control is achieved inherently by the design, as the CNV atmosphere is passively depleted of fission products.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system, which uses two independent trains of decay heat removal equipment, each capable of fully cooling the reactor. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, passive systems transfer decay heat to a large, stainless steel-lined reactor pool, ensuring continued cooling through natural circulation and heat rejection to the atmosphere or external sinks.

nuscale_ch01_0681.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.3 AssumptionsPostulated Steam Line Break Effects
nuscale_ch01_0681.summary: This section outlines NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for steam system piping failures both inside and outside containment, specifically addressing the assumptions related to postulated steam line break effects. It confirms that the analysis meets all relevant criteria, including initial power level, plant operating mode, loss of offsite power, and worst-case single active component failure, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0680.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.2 AssumptionsLoss of Offsite Power
nuscale_ch01_0680.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for steam system piping failures inside and outside containment, specifically under the assumption of loss of offsite power. It confirms that NuScale meets all relevant criteria in DSRS 15.1.5, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, addressing events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, prevention of escalation from anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), and use of appropriate instrument setpoints, aligning with Standard Review Plan requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operating occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with relevant standards, including RG 1.105 and the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0108.title: 8.37 ALARA Levels for Effluents from Materials Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0108.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, specifically for single-line diagrams. It details the meaning of various symbols and notations for components such as transformers, circuit breakers, batteries, switches, and other electrical devices, ensuring clarity and consistency in interpreting the diagrams.

nuscale_ch01_0109.title: Figure 1.7-1a: Electrical Symbols
nuscale_ch01_0109.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols used in NuScale's single-line diagrams, detailing the meaning of various symbols and the information associated with each, such as ratings, voltage levels, and device types. It explains how to interpret symbols for transformers, circuit breakers, batteries, switches, and other electrical components, ensuring clarity in reading and understanding the diagrams.

nuscale_ch01_0413.title: SRP 9.5.7, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Lubrication SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4) Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0413.summary: The NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the regulatory criteria outlined in SRP 9.5.7 (and related sections) are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on AC or DC power, making compliance with GDC 2, 4, 5, and 17 for these systems unnecessary.

nuscale_ch01_0412.title: SRP 9.5.6, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Starting SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4)Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0412.summary: The NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the regulatory criteria in SRP 9.5.6 (and related sections) regarding emergency diesel engine starting systems are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on AC or DC power, making compliance with GDC 2, 4, 5, and 17 for these systems unnecessary.
